sections.industry_use_cases:
1) Scotiabank (Commercial Banking): Agentic AI added to AIDox to process commercial client emails autonomously, route them to correct teams, and create cases—reducing client wait times.[Scotiabank Perspectives (Agentic AI)](https://www.scotiabank.com/ca/en/about/perspectives.articles.digital.2025-07-agentic-ai-scotiabank-artificial-intelligence.html)

2) Scotiabank (Derivatives ops): AIDox used to confirm over-the-counter derivative trades, reducing turnaround time by up to 1.5 hours (with human review remaining).[Scotiabank Perspectives (Agentic AI)](https://www.scotiabank.com/ca/en/about/perspectives.articles.digital.2025-07-agentic-ai-scotiabank-artificial-intelligence.html)

3) Scotiabank (Employee assist): AskAI chatbot built using Google’s Gemini to help contact-centre agents and branch employees answer banking/service questions faster.[Scotiabank Perspectives (Agentic AI)](https://www.scotiabank.com/ca/en/about/perspectives.articles.digital.2025-07-agentic-ai-scotiabank-artificial-intelligence.html)

4) Scotiabank (Payments ops): Multi-agent system (built with Microsoft Copilot platform, with EY/Microsoft) automating data transformation, reconciliation, exception handling, and client report generation—demonstrating how agent systems can compress delivery timelines and scale premium services.[Scotiabank GTB Insights](https://gtb.scotiabank.com/en/global-transaction-banking/resources/insights/article.insights.how-ai-agents-are-transforming-scotiabank-s-payment-operations.html)

5) Broader commercial banking AI pattern: AI-enabled processes for pipeline qualification and cross-sell/upsell, with case-study references to deploying Watson-based processes and aiming to shift meaningful revenue through AI-enabled chat experiences.[Alexander Group](https://www.alexandergroup.com/insights/ai-use-cases-for-commercial-banking/)

name: Shane Leech-Porter, CFA
title: Chief Executive Officer (CEO), Kurnl
background:
Shane Leech-Porter is a Victoria, British Columbia–based executive and CFA charterholder who became CEO of Kurnl in September 2025, leading the launch of a Canadian open-access fibre company focused on multi-family buildings ([LinkedIn profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sl-p)).

At Kurnl, his stated focus is getting the “fundamentals right” (operations, financials, positioning, partner development) while building relationships with developers and internet service providers (ISPs) and bringing a transparent, developer-first proposition to market ([LinkedIn post on early Kurnl progress](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sl-p_realestatedevelopment-digitalinfrastructure-activity-7424106976009244672-lNuM)).

Kurnl positions itself as a turnkey designer/builder/operator of open-access fibre networks for multi-family buildings, aiming to create building revenue streams, enable neutral ISP competition, and provide residents “day-zero” connectivity; it also states BC and Alberta networks opening in 2026 and provides early network stats (active units, fibre-installed units, uptime, provider count) ([Kurnl website](https://www.kurnl.ca)).

Before Kurnl, Leech-Porter spent ~15 years at British Columbia Investment Management Corporation (BCI/bcIMC) in public markets/thematic investing leadership roles, including Managing Director, Global Thematic (2018–2025), and a period as Interim Senior Managing Director, Active Internal Equities (Aug 2021–Apr 2022) with responsibility for seven equity teams and ~C$20B AUM ([LinkedIn profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sl-p)).

In his BCI experience descriptions, he reports building and leading a seven-person Global Thematic team; chairing a multi-asset thematic advisory committee; serving on a senior investment advisory committee; and growing the Global Thematic program to a peak of ~$6.5B AUM with multiple internal clients adopting the program ([LinkedIn profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sl-p)).

Earlier career roles include equity research associate positions at TD Securities (2008–2010) and Desjardins Securities (2006–2008), investment banking analyst at BMO Capital Markets (2004–2005), and a finance internship at Terasen Inc. (summer 2003) where he managed large cash accounts via money market transactions ([LinkedIn profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sl-p)).

Education and credentials: Bachelor of Commerce (Honours), Finance, University of British Columbia (1999–2003) and CFA designation earned in the mid-2000s (as reflected in third-party aggregations and his profile name) ([ContactOut profile](https://contactout.com/Shane-Leech-porter-135401859); [LinkedIn profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sl-p)).

Public writing/talks: the most visible public footprint found is LinkedIn, where he posts about Kurnl’s positioning and his outreach to multi-family developers (including time spent meeting developers in Vancouver leading to invitations to submit proposals) ([LinkedIn post on Vancouver developer meetings](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sl-p_realestatedevelopment-multifamily-rentalhousing-activity-7427004095711952897-4cyQ)).

Personal interests: beyond leadership themes (psychological safety, candor, long-term shared value) stated on LinkedIn, no reliable public sources surfaced describing hobbies, family, or extracurricular commitments ([LinkedIn profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sl-p)).

[Fiber Broadband Association AI + fiber white paper (PDF)](https://fiberbroadband.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/FBA-087_AI_WhitePaper_FIN.pdf)
[Turn-Key Technologies on AI predictive maintenance](https://www.turn-keytechnologies.com/blog/ai-in-predictive-maintenance-for-network-systems)

sections.ai_impact:
For an open-access fibre CEO, AI’s biggest operational impact is shifting network operations from manual/threshold-based monitoring to data-driven, increasingly autonomous operations (anomaly detection, root-cause reasoning, and self-healing remediation) to protect uptime while keeping operating cost low as footprint scales ([McKinsey on AI-driven telecom networks](https://www.mckinsey.com.br/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/issue-brief-ai-driven-telecom-networks)).

Because Kurnl’s model depends on high availability and low-friction ISP onboarding across many buildings, AI-enabled service assurance (detecting emerging issues before residents notice) becomes a differentiator that supports both resident experience and ISP partner satisfaction ([Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada principles for AI adoption in telecom](https://ised-isde.canada.ca/site/ised/en/principles-artificial-intelligence-adoption-telecommunications-industry)).

AI can also directly improve field operations by forecasting faults (predictive maintenance) and optimizing technician dispatch windows, reducing emergency truck rolls and increasing the reliability metrics Kurnl markets to developers and owners ([Turn-Key Technologies on AI predictive maintenance](https://www.turn-keytechnologies.com/blog/ai-in-predictive-maintenance-for-network-systems)).

Commercially, AI changes demand dynamics: hyperscale AI and data-center buildouts are increasing fiber intensity and placing a premium on scalable fibre supply chains and AI-ready connectivity architectures; that macro tailwind is relevant for strategic positioning, partnerships, and long-term capex planning even for a building-focused open-access operator ([Fiber Broadband Association AI + fiber white paper](https://fiberbroadband.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/FBA-087_AI_WhitePaper_FIN.pdf)).

Key challenges AI can address for his role: (1) scaling operations without linear headcount growth (AIOps, automated ticket triage, automated change-risk analysis), (2) compressing time-to-serve (faster proposals, standardized designs, faster ISP onboarding), (3) proving reliability and ROI to developers with credible, explainable metrics, and (4) governance—deploying AI in ways that are safe, secure, and trustworthy (privacy, security, and IP respect), which is explicitly emphasized in Canadian telecom AI-adoption guidance ([Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada principles for AI adoption in telecom](https://ised-isde.canada.ca/site/ised/en/principles-artificial-intelligence-adoption-telecommunications-industry)).
sections.ai_tools:
1) **AIOps / Service assurance copilots**: AI assistants that correlate alerts, logs, tickets, and topology to accelerate root-cause analysis and remediation—useful for maintaining Kurnl’s uptime promise as more buildings come online (e.g., “smart co-pilots and root-cause models” described in telecom issue-management journeys) ([McKinsey on AI-driven telecom networks](https://www.mckinsey.com.br/industries/technology-media-and-communications/our-insights/issue-brief-ai-driven-telecom-networks)).

2) **Predictive maintenance analytics**: ML systems that forecast equipment or link degradation from operational telemetry so maintenance can be scheduled proactively, reducing downtime and emergency repairs in distributed networks ([Turn-Key Technologies on AI predictive maintenance](https://www.turn-keytechnologies.com/blog/ai-in-predictive-maintenance-for-network-systems)).

3) **AI-assisted proposal + contracting workflows**: GenAI drafting tools (for RFP responses, builder/developer proposals, ISP/MSA drafts, technical appendices) to shorten cycles; the value is highest where Kurnl is repeatedly customizing a core proposition for different developers and buildings (grounded in his LinkedIn-described focus on partner engagement and proposals) ([LinkedIn post on Vancouver developer meetings](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sl-p_realestatedevelopment-multifamily-rentalhousing-activity-7427004095711952897-4cyQ)).

4) **Customer/support automation with strong privacy controls**: GenAI support assistants for installation day-zero issues, resident onboarding, and ISP coordination—aligned with telecom emphasis on improving customer experience while adopting AI safely and securely ([Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada principles for AI adoption in telecom](https://ised-isde.canada.ca/site/ised/en/principles-artificial-intelligence-adoption-telecommunications-industry)).

5) **Knowledge management + engineering search**: Internal “network bible” Q&A that ingests as-builts, standard designs, ISP integration runbooks, and past incidents—directly accelerates onboarding and reduces single points of failure (supported by the broader trend toward AI copilots for issue management) ([McKinsey on AI-driven telecom networks](https://www.mckinsey.com.br/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/issue-brief-ai-driven-telecom-networks)).
sections.agentic_frameworks:
1) **AIOps agents for incident lifecycle automation**: Multi-step agents that (a) detect anomalies, (b) gather context (topology, recent changes), (c) propose remediation, and (d) open/route tickets to the right human with a ready-to-execute runbook—mirroring the AI-enabled “issue management journey” described for telcos ([McKinsey on AI-driven telecom networks](https://www.mckinsey.com.br/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/issue-brief-ai-driven-telecom-networks)).

2) **Change-risk + rollout agents**: Agents that review planned network changes (firmware, config, ISP integration) for disruption risk and propose rollback/mitigation plans, consistent with “critical change agents” described in telecom AI operations ([McKinsey on AI-driven telecom networks](https://www.mckinsey.com.br/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/issue-brief-ai-driven-telecom-networks)).

3) **RevOps / partnership pipeline agents**: Agents that maintain a structured CRM view of developer and ISP accounts, summarize last interactions, draft follow-ups, and generate meeting briefs—high leverage given his current focus on partner development and proposals ([LinkedIn post on Vancouver developer meetings](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sl-p_realestatedevelopment-multifamily-rentalhousing-activity-7427004095711952897-4cyQ)).

4) **Design-to-deploy workflow automation**: Agentic checklists for each building deployment (design review → permitting artifacts → construction coordination → ISP onboarding → resident launch) to reduce variance across projects, aligned with Kurnl’s positioning as turnkey delivery ([Kurnl website](https://www.kurnl.ca)).
sections.role_transformation:
- **Network reliability management**: Moving from reactive incident response to predictive detection and partially automated remediation to sustain very high uptime while scaling footprint ([Turn-Key Technologies on AI predictive maintenance](https://www.turn-keytechnologies.com/blog/ai-in-predictive-maintenance-for-network-systems); [McKinsey on AI-driven telecom networks](https://www.mckinsey.com.br/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/issue-brief-ai-driven-telecom-networks)).

- **Partner enablement and proposals**: Faster creation of developer proposals and clearer articulation of building-level ROI and value proposition—directly relevant to the work he describes doing with multi-family developers and the invitations to submit proposals ([LinkedIn post on Vancouver developer meetings](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sl-p_realestatedevelopment-multifamily-rentalhousing-activity-7427004095711952897-4cyQ)).

- **Operational scaling**: Standardizing and automating the recurring “building launch” process (design, installation coordination, ISP onboarding, resident onboarding) so growth does not require linear headcount increases ([Kurnl website](https://www.kurnl.ca)).

- **Security and trust**: More sophisticated anomaly detection for security/fraud, plus governance practices to deploy AI in ways that are safe and trustworthy—an explicit industry emphasis in Canada’s telecom AI-adoption principles ([Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada principles for AI adoption in telecom](https://ised-isde.canada.ca/site/ised/en/principles-artificial-intelligence-adoption-telecommunications-industry)).
sections.learning_resources:
- **Canadian telecom AI governance and trust**: Principles document on safe, secure, trustworthy AI adoption in telecom, useful as a baseline for AI governance and partner/customer trust ([Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada principles for AI adoption in telecom](https://ised-isde.canada.ca/site/ised/en/principles-artificial-intelligence-adoption-telecommunications-industry)).

- **Telecom network operations transformation**: Practical overview of AI across issue management, self-healing, and operations redesign ([McKinsey on AI-driven telecom networks](https://www.mckinsey.com.br/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/issue-brief-ai-driven-telecom-networks)).

- **Fiber + AI demand dynamics**: How AI workloads and architectures increase fibre requirements and shape “AI-ready” fibre strategies (useful for long-term strategic context and investor conversations) ([Fiber Broadband Association AI + fiber white paper](https://fiberbroadband.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/FBA-087_AI_WhitePaper_FIN.pdf)).
sections.industry_use_cases:
- **AI-native telecom operations (issue management, self-healing)**: Operators are using AI for advanced anomaly detection, root-cause reasoning copilots, and automated remediation and ticket routing to reduce outage duration and operating cost ([McKinsey on AI-driven telecom networks](https://www.mckinsey.com.br/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/issue-brief-ai-driven-telecom-networks)).

- **Predictive maintenance in telecom networks**: AI models analyze telemetry from network elements to predict failures, allowing proactive maintenance scheduling and reduced downtime in large, distributed networks ([Turn-Key Technologies on AI predictive maintenance](https://www.turn-keytechnologies.com/blog/ai-in-predictive-maintenance-for-network-systems)).

- **AI driving fibre infrastructure buildout**: AI data center expansion is increasing fibre intensity and prompting new AI-oriented fibre strategies—an industry tailwind shaping investment and partnership decisions across fibre ecosystems ([Fiber Broadband Association AI + fiber white paper](https://fiberbroadband.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/FBA-087_AI_WhitePaper_FIN.pdf)).

sections.ai_impact:
As founder/CEO of a small, senior-heavy frontend development shop, Troy sits at the intersection of delivery (shipping performant sites), client advisory (stack choices/migrations), and talent management; AI’s biggest impacts will be (1) compressing implementation time, (2) changing what clients “buy” from agencies, and (3) increasing the importance of architecture, evaluation, and trust.

1) Delivery compression + higher expectations
- AI-assisted coding will reduce time for routine tasks (component scaffolding, test generation, content migrations, docs), letting Gearbox deliver more per engineer; in parallel, client expectations will rise (faster turnaround, more experimentation, more personalization).

2) Differentiation shifts from “code output” to “system design + outcomes”
- As LLMs make it easier for non-experts to generate code and pages, Gearbox’s advantage becomes: information architecture, performance budgets, accessibility, analytics/experimentation design, and reliable operations.
- The agency will increasingly sell “decision quality” (stack selection, migration sequencing, content modeling, security posture) and “velocity systems” (design system + CI/CD + preview workflows), consistent with Gearbox’s positioning around performance and dev-first workflows. ([Vercel blog](https://vercel.com/blog/incremental-migration-from-wordpress-for-a-dev-first-approach))

3) Exponential tech opportunities in modern web stacks
- Edge compute + AI inference at the edge can enable personalization, content classification, and routing without heavy origin infrastructure—an extension of Gearbox’s existing use of edge middleware patterns described by Vercel. ([Vercel blog](https://vercel.com/blog/incremental-migration-from-wordpress-for-a-dev-first-approach))

4) Key challenges AI can address for Troy/Gearbox
- Estimation & scoping: turning messy requirements into clear backlogs, identifying risks/unknowns early.
- Migrations: accelerating content audits, URL mapping, redirect generation, and QA checks.
- Quality: automated accessibility checks, regression detection, performance analysis, and test coverage.
- Sales/marketing: generating tailored proposals, case studies, and vertical landing pages with consistent positioning.

5) Risks to manage
- IP/security: preventing confidential client code/content from leaking to model providers; choosing enterprise controls or self-hosted options where needed.
- Quality drift: AI-generated code that passes review but accumulates technical debt; need guardrails (linting, tests, architectural patterns).
- Reputation risk: hallucinated claims in marketing content; require citation/verification workflows.

6) Near-term strategic bets
- Productize “migration playbooks” (WordPress → headless; Shopify → headless) with automation and repeatable checklists.
- Build an internal “Gearbox AI copilot” set of prompts, code templates, and evaluation harnesses tuned to the team’s stack (Next.js, Sanity, Vercel). ([Troy McGinnis LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/troymcginnis)) ([Vercel blog](https://vercel.com/blog/incremental-migration-from-wordpress-for-a-dev-first-approach))
sections.ai_tools:
Engineering + delivery (directly relevant to modern frontend work)
- GitHub Copilot: code completion and refactoring inside the IDE; strongest ROI for TypeScript/React/Next.js teams through scaffolding and iteration.
- Cursor: AI-first code editor for fast multi-file edits (useful for migrations, refactors, and content model changes).
- Sourcegraph Cody: codebase-aware chat and change suggestions across larger repos (handy when supporting multiple client codebases).

Testing, QA, and accessibility
- Playwright + AI assist (via Copilot/Cursor): accelerate test writing for Next.js apps and critical flows.
- axe DevTools: accessibility testing and guided remediation (AI can help interpret and fix issues faster).

Content and SEO workflow
- ChatGPT / Claude: requirements distillation, information architecture drafts, redirect-map generation, SEO brief generation, and stakeholder-ready summaries (with strict verification rules).
- Perplexity: research assistant for tech selection comparisons, client vertical research, and competitive teardowns (with source-linked outputs).

Design-to-code / asset creation
- Figma AI (when available in their workflow): speeds up component exploration and pattern creation for design systems.

Operations + client comms
- Fireflies / Otter: meeting transcription + action items for client calls, turning discussions into trackable tasks.
- Notion AI: drafting proposals/SOWs, turning notes into structured plans; works well if Notion is used as a delivery hub.

Note: Tool selection should be matched to client confidentiality constraints; for regulated clients, prefer enterprise plans or self-hosted options and explicitly exclude sensitive repos from training wherever possible.
sections.agentic_frameworks:
Developer-focused agent frameworks (build internal automations)
- OpenAI Agents SDK: create tool-using agents for tasks like generating redirect maps, drafting migration runbooks, or producing release notes.
- LangGraph: orchestration for multi-step, stateful agent workflows (e.g., audit → plan → implement → test → report) with human review gates.

Team workflow automation
- n8n: self-hostable workflow automation to connect GitHub/Vercel/Sanity/Slack for release notifications, content QA, and incident workflows.
- Zapier: fast automation for smaller teams (e.g., lead → proposal → kickoff pipeline).

DevOps / code-review automation
- Renovate: automated dependency updates (pair with AI-generated PR descriptions and risk notes).
- Danger (CI): enforce PR checks and standards; pair with AI to generate checklists per project type.

Customer support / docs agents (for clients)
- Intercom Fin / Zendesk AI: for clients needing support deflection; Gearbox can offer implementation + content modeling as a service.

Website “agentic” experiences (client-facing)
- Chat/assist widgets grounded on a client’s docs/knowledge base (RAG) for product marketing sites; can reduce support load and increase conversion when implemented carefully with guardrails.

Practical “agent recipes” Gearbox could build
- Migration Agent: crawls a sitemap export + CMS export → proposes content model mapping → generates redirect rules → outputs QA checklist.
- Performance Agent: runs Lighthouse/CrUX checks → proposes code/config changes → opens PR with fixes and measured deltas.
- SEO Agent: checks metadata/schema/OG tags → drafts improvements aligned to brand voice → generates PR + changelog.
sections.role_transformation:
For a founder/CEO who is also deeply technical, AI will transform:

Delivery and engineering
- Estimation/scoping: converting loosely defined goals into a backlog, identifying edge cases early, and generating spike plans.
- Implementation: faster component generation, refactors, and integration glue code for Next.js/Sanity/Vercel stacks. ([Troy McGinnis LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/troymcginnis))
- Migrations: automating URL inventories, redirect rule generation, and content transformation when moving from WordPress to headless approaches. ([Vercel blog](https://vercel.com/blog/incremental-migration-from-wordpress-for-a-dev-first-approach))
- QA: generating tests, synthesizing bug repro steps, and improving accessibility/performance remediation.

Client advisory and strategy
- Competitive/tech research: faster synthesis of platform tradeoffs; better justification decks for headless vs traditional CMS.
- Stakeholder communication: turning technical constraints into clear, non-technical narratives (what changes, why now, and the ROI).

Business development + operations
- Proposal/SOW drafting: faster generation of tailored statements of work, project plans, and timelines.
- Hiring and talent: more standardized technical screening rubrics and onboarding docs; evaluating AI-assisted candidates.

New service lines
- “AI-ready” web builds: instrumented sites built for experimentation/personalization and safe AI features (chat, content assistants, internal search).
sections.learning_resources:
Modern web + AI (high-signal, practitioner oriented)
- Vercel blog (especially posts combining Next.js, edge patterns, and production workflows), aligned to Gearbox’s documented stack. ([Vercel blog](https://vercel.com/blog/incremental-migration-from-wordpress-for-a-dev-first-approach))

Agentic systems / applied LLM engineering
- DeepLearning.AI short courses (e.g., LLM app building, agent workflows) for rapidly operationalizing agent patterns.
- “LLM University” style content from leading platforms (OpenAI/Anthropic docs and cookbooks) to standardize team practices.

Product + experimentation (useful for marketing-site outcomes)
- Reforge essays / experimentation content (if available) and growth experimentation resources to pair AI-generated variants with disciplined measurement.

Podcasts/newsletters (practitioner-friendly)
- Latent Space (newsletter/podcast): LLM engineering, tooling, and product patterns.
- Software Engineering Daily (selected AI + infra episodes): keeps perspective on production realities.
- Practical AI (podcast): steady coverage of applied AI.

Books (conceptual + practical)
- Designing Machine Learning Systems (Chip Huyen): useful for understanding production ML constraints even if Gearbox isn’t training models.
- Accelerate (Forsgren/Humble/Kim): complements AI acceleration with delivery discipline (CI/CD, metrics).
sections.industry_use_cases:
Web agencies and modern web stacks are using AI in repeatable ways that map closely to Gearbox’s work:

1) Faster site builds and migrations
- Agencies use AI to accelerate WordPress-to-headless migrations by generating redirect maps, transforming content exports, and drafting content models; this aligns with Gearbox’s incremental migration approach described by Vercel. ([Vercel blog](https://vercel.com/blog/incremental-migration-from-wordpress-for-a-dev-first-approach))

2) Performance and SEO automation
- Teams increasingly pair automated performance testing (Lighthouse/field data checks) with AI-assisted remediation to meet performance budgets, particularly for marketing teams that need rapid iteration (Gearbox’s explicit positioning). ([Gearbox Development Inc. LinkedIn](https://ca.linkedin.com/company/gearboxbuilt))

3) Content operations for marketing teams
- Marketing orgs use AI to generate on-brand landing page copy variants, then run experimentation; agencies that provide the measurement/instrumentation layer differentiate beyond copy generation.

4) Support deflection + product education on websites
- Companies deploy AI chat over their docs/knowledge bases to reduce support tickets and increase conversion; agencies often implement the retrieval grounding, guardrails, and analytics.

5) Internal agency ops
- Small agencies use AI for proposal generation, meeting notes, and project status reporting, allowing founders to spend more time on client relationships and delivery quality.
